task: I think I may have run into something suspicious with a recent bank message, and I don’t want to overreact or ignore it. Could you help me sort out the safest next steps and what I should check first?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — safest approach is to treat it as suspicious until you verify it.

First, **don’t click any links or reply to the message**. If it includes a phone number, don’t use that either unless you can confirm it from the bank’s official website or the back of your card.

What I’d check first:
- **Sender details**: exact email address, phone number, or short code
- **Message wording**: urgency, threats, odd grammar, or requests to “verify” info
- **Links**: hover if you can, or press and hold to see the real destination
- **Requests for personal info**: password, PIN, full card number, code, or SSN
- **Account changes**: claims of locked account, unusual transaction, or “refund” notices

Safest next steps:
1. **Open your bank’s official app or website directly** by typing it yourself.
2. **Check recent activity** there, not through the message.
3. **Call the bank using the number on your card or official site** if anything looks off.
4. **Save the message** and take screenshots before deleting anything.
5. If you clicked something or entered info, **change your bank password right away** and ask the bank to review the account.

What I’d write down, just in case:
- date and time of the message
- exact sender info
- any phone number or link shown
- what it claimed
- whether you clicked anything or replied

If you want, paste the message text here with any personal info removed, and I can help you judge whether it looks like a scam.

user_simulator.text:
That sounds sensible. I’d want to know one thing first — what exactly did the message say? Even just the wording, with any account numbers or your name taken out. I’ve learned the hard way not to rush when something feels off. Last time I ignored a warning sign, it cost me money and a lot of fuss with the bank.

If it’s easier, you can also tell me:
- was it text, email, or a phone call
- did it say your account was locked or that there was a charge
- was there a link or phone number in it
- did it ask for a code or password

Then I can help you sort out whether it’s just noise or something to call the bank about right away.

turns.agent_response:
That’s the right way to handle it.

If you can, send me the **exact wording** of the message with your name, account number, codes, and any personal details removed. Also tell me:
- **text, email, or call**
- whether it said **locked account, suspicious charge, refund, or transfer**
- whether there was a **link or phone number**
- whether it asked for a **code, password, or PIN**

In the meantime, safest move is:
1. **Don’t click, call, or reply** from the message itself.
2. **Open the bank app or type the bank’s website yourself**.
3. **Check recent activity** for anything unfamiliar.
4. If anything seems off, **call the bank using the number on your card or official site**.

If you paste the wording here, I’ll help you sort it out carefully.

user_simulator.text:
All right, here’s what it said, more or less:

"Security Alert: We detected unusual activity on your checking account. Your account has been temporarily limited. Please verify your identity to restore access by clicking the link below or call us immediately at 1-800-555-0148. Do not share this message with anyone."

It was a text message. There was a link in it too, but I didn’t open it. It didn’t ask for my password outright, but it did mention a verification code. That part made me uneasy.

I’ve had one of these before, and once they start with the hurry-up business, I get suspicious pretty quick now.

turns.agent_response:
That does sound suspicious, and you did the right thing by not opening the link.

A few red flags there:
- **generic “Security Alert” wording**
- **pressure to act immediately**
- **link in the text**
- **request for a verification code**
- **phone number in the message itself** instead of one you already know is official

Safest next steps:
1. **Do not click the link or call the number in the text.**
2. **Open your bank app directly** or type the bank’s official website yourself.
3. **Check recent transactions and account status** there.
4. If you see anything odd, **call the bank using the number on the back of your card or the official website**.
5. If the message came from a short code or sender you can block, **save screenshots first, then block/report it**.

About the verification code:
- **Do not share any code** with anyone who contacted you by text.
- A real bank may send codes only after **you** start a login or recovery process through the official app/site.

If you already tapped the link or entered anything, tell me that right away — the next steps would be different. If not, I can help you draft a quick call script for the bank.

user_simulator.text:
Mm-hm. That’s exactly the sort of thing I’d treat as a scam. The big giveaway is that it’s trying to rush you, and that verification code part is the dangerous piece.

What I’d do, plain and simple:
- don’t click the link
- don’t call that number
- go straight into the bank app yourself, or type the bank website in by hand
- check whether your account actually has any alerts or unusual activity
- if anything looks wrong, call the number on the back of your card

And whatever you do, don’t give out any code from a text. I’ve had the same kind of hurry-up message before, and once they start pressing you like that, it’s usually trouble.

If you want the safest next step right this minute: open the app from your phone the usual way and look for any notice there. Not from the text. From the app itself.
